objects.description: This defines PAP as a STIX extension

objects.name: Odido Data Extortion
objects.description: ShinyHunters leaked a massive dataset from the Dutch telecom provider Odido, impacting 6.1 million users. The leak includes sensitive data such as IBANs, passport numbers, and internal Salesforce records.

objects.content:
Sector: Telecommunications

Modus operandi:
Data theft from CRM systems (Salesforce), followed by public leak on Tor sites to pressure the victim.

objects.name: Vishing Supergroup Recruitment
objects.description: A supergroup formed by Lapsus$, Scattered Spider, and ShinyHunters is recruiting female callers for high-stakes vishing attacks targeting corporate IT help desks.

objects.content:
Sector: Cross-sector (Corporate IT)

Modus operandi:
Social engineering via voice calls to bypass MFA and reset credentials, using gender-specific scripts to disarm help desk staff.

objects.name: EncystPHP FreePBX Exploitation
objects.description: Exploitation of CVE-2025-64328 to deploy the EncystPHP web shell on Sangoma FreePBX instances worldwide.

objects.name: CVE-2025-64328
objects.description: Vulnerability CVE-2025-64328 referenced in threat report.

objects.content:
Sector: VoIP / IT Services

Modus operandi:
Command injection via endpoint manager, installation of PHP-based web shells for persistence and root access.

objects.name: Breach: Odido
objects.description:
Data breach affecting 6.1 million users, exposing names, addresses, phone numbers, IBANs, and passport details.

Sector: Telecommunications

objects.name: Breach: KomikoAI

Leak of 1.1 million accounts including AI prompts, emails, and forum posts.

Sector: Technology / AI

objects.abstract: Geopolitical analysis (EN) / AI Weaponization in Mexico
objects.content:
Sector: Government

Theme: AI Weaponization in Mexico

Hackers utilized Anthropic’s Claude Code and OpenAI’s GPT-4 to automate data exfiltration and exploit generation against 10 Mexican government agencies.

Tags: Mexico, AI Abuse, Espionage

objects.abstract: Geopolitical analysis (EN) / South Korean Police Data Leak
objects.content:
Sector: Government / Finance

Theme: South Korean Police Data Leak

South Korean authorities accidentally exposed mnemonic phrases for seized crypto-wallets in a high-resolution press release photograph, leading to loss of funds.

Tags: South Korea, OPSEC failure, Crypto

objects.name: ClawJacked Attack on OpenClaw AI Agents
objects.description:
High-severity flaw (ClawJacked) allows malicious websites to silently brute-force local OpenClaw instances via WebSockets, bypassing rate limiting due to localhost exemptions.

Analysis:
The vulnerability leverages the default binding of the gateway to localhost. Since browser cross-origin policies don't block localhost WebSockets, an external site can act as a bridge to the local agent.

Recommendations:
- Upgrade OpenClaw to version 2026.2.26 or later.
- Avoid running AI agents with default authentication on exposed local ports.

objects.name: EncystPHP Web Shell Campaign
objects.description:
Malicious campaign targeting Sangoma FreePBX instances to install the EncystPHP dropper and maintain persistence through root-level users.

Analysis:
The campaign is highly automated, deleting logs and rival web shells upon installation to ensure exclusive control over the compromised PBX.

Recommendations:
- Block the IP 45.234.176.202 and domain crm.razatelefonia.pro.
- Audit FreePBX instances for unauthorized root users or modified cron jobs.

objects.name: Minerva Discord Token Stealer
objects.description:
A malicious script (minerva.py) embedded in piracy-related websites designed to steal Discord authentication tokens using OAuth redirection.

Analysis:
The script uses OAuth flows to capture tokens, which are then transmitted to non-Discord servers, potentially acting as a honeypot for legal tracking of piracy users.

Recommendations:
- Review Discord authorized applications and revoke access to 'Minerva Archive'.
- Monitor for the SHA256 hash in script executions.

objects.content: The cyber threat landscape for March 2, 2026, is dominated by the strategic weaponization of Artificial Intelligence and the emergence of cybercrime 'supergroups.' We observe a critical trend where generative AI (Claude Code) is being used to automate exploits against government infrastructure in Mexico. Simultaneously, the alliance between ShinyHunters, Lapsus$, and Scattered Spider (forming the 'Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters') indicates a shift toward hybrid operations combining sophisticated vishing with traditional data extortion. Vulnerability research shows a massive wave of critical flaws in network appliances (Tenda) and specialized AI/dev tools (Langflow, Angular SSR), emphasizing that the supply chain of both IoT and modern development frameworks remains a high-value target for remote code execution.
